entry.title: Washington Post: 29 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Moscow move to shutter Jewish Agency alarms Russian Jews
  • Slain journalist’s family puts spotlight on Israeli impunity
  • Ukraine Live Briefing: Russia noncommittal on Griner deal; safe passage for grain still being arranged
  • Biden talks with China’s Xi amid tension over Taiwan
  • Pope Francis visits a Quebec that’s rapidly shedding its Catholicism
  • Human-caused climate change made U.K. heat wave 10 times more likely, study says
  • Saudi crown prince engages in long handshake with Macron on rehabilitation tour
  • James Lovelock, creator of Gaia theory of ‘living’ Earth, dies at 103
  • New WHO monkeypox advice urges men who have sex with men to limit partners
  • Saudi crown prince wants you talking about his ‘city of the future’
  • What to know about Rishi Sunak and Liz Truss, finalists to be U.K. prime minister
  • Ukraine could be turning the tide of war again as Russian advances stall
  • Climate change is killing more elephants than poaching, Kenyan officials say
  • Nuclear threat higher now than in Cold War, British official warns
  • U.S. officials hope public pressure will bring Russian release of prisoners
  • Proposal for Griner, Whelan follows history of U.S.-Russia prisoner swaps
  • Ancient Europeans were lactose intolerant. They drank milk anyway, study finds.
  • 5 dead, 68 Haitians rescued from waters near Puerto Rico
  • UN resolution recognizes right to clean, healthy environment
  • EXPLAINER: What's behind the storming of Iraq's parliament?
  • Memphis woman cremated in Fiji, husband charged with murder
  • UN extends Libya mission for 3 months at Russia's insistence
  • Falling screen hits 2 dancers on stage at Hong Kong concert
  • 94 migrants escape suffocation in truck in Mexico
  • Highway double take: Albuquerque sign spelled without 'R'
  • Man arrested with gun outside Rep. Jayapal’s home charged with stalking
  • Jan. 6 texts missing for Trump Homeland Security’s Wolf and Cuccinelli
  • FBI seeks to settle lawsuits over bungled Larry Nassar investigation
  • The Claremont Institute triumphed in the Trump years. Then came Jan. 6.
  • Girl, 12, challenges W.Va. lawmakers on abortion: ‘What about my life?’
  • Female inmates raped after guard sold key to their cells, lawsuit says
  • In viral ad, doctor calls Texas governor to get permission for abortion
  • Employees at a fruit stand found a wallet. It was William Shatner’s.
  • How 80 dogs were saved: ‘We’ve done a lot of rescues ... but never one like this’
  • Man accused in plot to kill Kavanaugh eyed other justices, FBI says
  • Post Politics Now: Biden rejects claims that U.S. is in a recession
  • Raising Cane’s drops $100,000 on Mega Millions tickets — again
  • Drug industry poised for rare political loss on prices
  • JetBlue agrees to buy Spirit in $3.8 billion deal
  • Trader Joe’s workers vote to unionize for the first time
  • Hershey warns 'trick-or-treat’ supply chain may cause candy shortage
  • Mortgage rates are brought down by recession fears
  • One woman sharing workplace bathrooms with 50 men
  • The MTV VMAs debut category for best virtual and video game concerts
  • Your phone has tools to help you use it less. They work.
  • This topsy-turvy housing market can help burned-out buyers reset
  • Environmentally friendly apartments coming to Hyattsville, Md.
  • Is Russia Winning the Battle for African Support?
  • Paris Faces an Even Colder, Darker Winter Than Berlin
  • How a Declining British City Changed Its Fortune
  • Europe’s Energy Plan Isn’t Nearly Ambitious Enough
  • What It Would Take for Jack Ma’s Ant to Reboot an IPO
  • What to Know About Bolsonaro-Lula Showdown in Brazil
  • Why Poor Nations Now Face a Pandemic Debt Crunch
  • What You Need to Know About Recessions — Including Whether We’re in One
  • Russia locked up Vladimir Kara-Murza for telling the truth about Ukraine
  • The surprise Manchin deal is cause for celebration
  • Biden is showing that governing from the middle is possible
  • Democrats prepare to scapegoat the Fed for their own follies
  • Joe Manchin’s climate deal isn’t enough, but it’s still a miracle
  • The Manchin deal should fail. But Republicans can learn something from it.
  • The Zelenskys are in Vogue. That makes them smart, not silly.
  • Despite evidence of innocence, officials leave Alabama man on death row
  • A Trump nomination is in no one’s interest. Democrats included.
  • Democrats, stop boosting election deniers in GOP primaries
  • A court’s ruling against Sandmann doesn’t vindicate media outlets
  • What the fall of WWE’s Vince McMahon reveals about post-Trump politics
  • I’m a drag queen. Here’s what my art really is.
  • Here’s how states can empower citizens to help combat gun violence
  • Still think this isn’t about control over women?
  • Out of bounds
  • Reactions to the Pope’s apology show Canadians are over symbolism
  • The risks of underwater breath-holding
  • An egregious case of both-siding
  • For young and old, the pandemic was upending
  • Trump’s impeachment lawyers knew or should have known better
  • The line the Lerners must not cross
  • California wants to help me buy a house. Here’s why that’s a bad idea.
  • Ask Eugene Robinson about the latest news around the U.S. and world
  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: Sydney Morning Herald: 29 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: Sydney Morning Herald: 29 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-29 07:32AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Race-by-race preview and tips for Rosehill on Saturday
  • ‘We live in a democracy, not a technocracy’: Kelly defends mask decisions
  • Why the Sea Eagles will dig their heels in on Taupau’s release request
  • McCormack ‘proud’ of regional money carve-up despite damning audit
  • Commonwealth Games 2022 LIVE updates: Competition set to begin with swimmers, rugby sevens and Diamonds ready to play
  • ‘Not colony nor province’: Zelensky marks statehood day as Russian forces attack Kyiv outskirts
  • Another Vogue cover, another controversy, this time in Ukraine
  • Australia news LIVE: Real wages not expected to rise until later this decade; Labor’s climate bill negotiations continue
  • Indigenous Voice deserves bipartisan support
  • Coach Langer turns critic, to call cricket for Seven
  • Have online sleuths put Poirot on the scrapheap?
  • Nick Kyrgios’ court date postponed by three weeks
  • Tigers hire a leading silk to fight Cowboys result
  • Federal Parliament's first week 2022
  • Best of cartoons, July 29, 2022
  • The latest illustrations from artist Alan Moir
  • Relevance bites in new parliamentary order
  • Train your own nurses, Australia told amid global shortage
  • Labor’s climate target will help drive businesses’ green power shift: Rio Tinto
  • Chalmers delivers bad economic news well, but can he hold the public’s trust?
  • Swearing allegiance to the Queen ‘ridiculous’: Republic minister
  • Real wages won’t rise for years as inflation bites: Chalmers
  • IMF downgrades growth in Australia and across Asia-Pacific
  • Greens elders Brown and Milne say emissions target debate not as important as coal exports
  • Liberal MPs opposing Voice to parliament because of a lack of detail
  • Sanitiser is little help to Ukraine. Nobody defeats Putin with clean hands
  • Average electricity price tripled to an all-time high last quarter
  • The latest illustrations from artist Matt Golding
  • The latest illustrations from artist Cathy Wilcox
  • The latest illustrations from artist Simon Letch
  • The latest illustrations from artist Andrew Dyson
  • Best of cartoons, July 28, 2022
  • Irrigators ‘gifted’ water licences by NSW ministers, communities claim
  • Modern cities like Sydney becoming ‘ghettos of rich people’, architects warn
  • Man charged with murdering elderly mother using pot plant
  • ‘They said it couldn’t be done’: Pokie-free Petersham Bowlo thrives at 125
  • Three teens charged with murder of 16-year-old in Sydney’s west after ‘fight over girl’
  • The Sydney suburbs where the most children received COVID-19 fines
  • Ayres played role in shortlisting candidates for NY trade job, previously secret documents reveal
  • Solve teacher shortage with fair work conditions
  • From the Archives, 1972: Australian is Miss Universe
  • In the Herald: July 29, 1972
  • The Double-Headed Rook Wrench eyes a return
  • Revised plan for Blackwattle Bay revealed after fierce backlash
  • Dispute over rail entity dents trust between NSW Treasury and watchdog: report
  • Brazil grants permit to pave highway through heart of Amazon forest
  • ‘Which polling is that?’: Jacinda Ardern confident despite bumps
  • Gallery: The best photos from around the world
  • ‘Epicentre’ San Francisco declares emergency over monkeypox spread
  • Spain tells women not to worry about body image on the beach - backlash ensues
  • ‘Really wounded me’: US Supreme Court Justice Alito mocks global critics of abortion ruling
  • In call with Biden, China’s Xi warns against ‘playing with fire’ over Taiwan
  • Indonesia warns of perils of nuclear-powered submarines in submission to the UN
  • Bali records ‘zero’ foot and mouth but experts say disease lurks
  • Counter-attack leaves Russian troops ‘highly vulnerable’ in Kherson: UK
  • ‘I used to be emotional’: The Millennial media owners taking on Putin
  • ‘How do you grow an economy without young people?’: What falling birth rates mean
  • The striptease that made unlikely friends of Todd McKenney and Shane Jacobson
  • Is poking a tall person in the eye with an umbrella the height of rudeness?
  • Rediscovering the Hamptons – and that strange way the rich go to the beach
  • Bernard Cribbins: actor starred in Dr Who and The Railway Children
  • Council moves to protect 180 Brisbane houses from developers, wreckers
  • Air-con conman jailed after fleecing customers of $350,000
  • Origin Energy locks in coal to ease power crunch
  • Never mind home alone, interns arrive to empty offices
  • Let’s discuss turnover contagion, shift shock and great regret
  • The show must go on ... but what if it doesn’t?
  • Survey: print media portrayal of teachers doesn’t help recruitment
  • Actions have a positive impact on our school community
  • Upskilling programs provide smart start in new country
  • ‘They bit off more than they could chew’: F45 founder weighs in on turmoil
  • From the Ritz to a brush with jail, a billionaire’s slow demise
  • The downfall of Chinese property giant’s $658 million man
  • Russian oligarchs’ billionaire banker looks to sever his ties with the past
  • The Wrap: ASX snaps three-month losing streak with a winning July
  • The US just plunged into a recession. Or maybe it didn’t
  • ‘Inflation, huh?’ Your ultimate dinner party survival guide to the economy
  • Arrogant, insular, ignorant: Commonwealth Bank chief reflects on bank’s past failings
  • Coinbase eyes off sport sponsorships as it launches in Australia
  • Budget-conscious shoppers on Kogan’s radar
  • Chalmers should drop the sob story and outline his policies
  • What to read next: Life-affirming lessons and stylish fashion advice
  • A migrant’s struggle to conjure a normal life into existence
  • Whatever happened to the giants of poetry, and where are they today?
  • The inside story of a toxic food scene by one who knows
  • There’s a body, a murderer and a whole lot more to think about
  • The best things to see and do in Melbourne in August
  • The best things to see and do in Sydney in August
  • Why did music critics stop criticising musicians?
  • David Ireland, ‘giant of letters’ and three-time Miles Franklin winner, dies
  • Beyonce is back. How does her latest album compare to her best records?
  • Neighbours finale a ratings smash, drawing biggest numbers in over a decade
  • Instagram pauses rollout of TikTok-like features following global backlash
  • It’s an ‘us’ and ‘them’ world, and the consequences are often bloody
  • How bad is Twitter’s bot problem?
  • Big tech’s winners and losers as ad market slumps and recession bites
  • I felt dirty dropping that plastic, but would a politician even care?
Breaking news from Sydney, Australia and the world. Features the latest business, sport, entertainment, travel, lifestyle, and technology news.

entry.title: NY Times: 29 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • After Clash, Manchin and Schumer Rushed to Reset Climate and Tax Deal
  • Democrats’ Plan to Fight Inflation May Lower Costs Over Time
  • U.S. Economy Shows Another Decline, Fanning Recession Fears
  • Big Tech Is Proving Resilient as the Economy Cools
  • As Congress Debated Landmark China Bill, Beijing Surged Ahead
  • Biden and Xi Conduct Marathon Call During Time of Rising Tensions
  • 2016 Campaign Looms Large as Justice Dept. Pursues Jan. 6 Inquiry
  • 8 Dead in Kentucky Flash Floods
  • Climate Change Worsened Britain’s Heat Wave, Scientists Find
  • Why the U.S. Offered to Swap Griner for Bout, a Russian Arms Dealer
  • Biden Administration Plans to Offer Updated Booster Shots in September
  • In Monkeypox, Gay Men Confront a Crisis With Echoes of the Past
  • On Monkeypox, Health Secretary Says States ‘Need to Work With Us’
  • F.T.C. Chair Lina Khan Upends Antitrust Standards by Suing Meta
  • Mike Judge Brings Back ‘Beavis and Butt-Head’
  • I’m Ukraine’s Foreign Minister. Putin Must Be Stopped.
  • How Goes the War on Inflation?
  • Finally, a Dr. Oz Show That I Really Want to Watch
  • These Republican Governors Are Delivering Results, and Many Voters Like Them for It
  • The Final Frontier Soon May No Longer Belong to All of Us

  • This 150-Year-Old Mining Law Hurts Taxpayers and the Environment
  • Four Fitness Facts to Fuel Your Workout
  • The Magic of Pizza Bread Crumbs
  • How Los Angeles Times Handled Exposé Becomes the Talk of the Town
  • Germans Tip-Toe Up the Path to Energy Savings
  • Ukraine Live Updates: Countries Across Europe Prepare for Winter Energy Squeeze
  • Crops ‘Stored Everywhere’: Ukraine’s Harvest Piles Up

  • Pope Francis, Slowed by Aging, Finds Lessons in Frailty
  • Iran Ramps Up Drone Exports, Signaling Global Ambitions
  • The Hunt Is On for ‘War Trophies’ in Ukraine
  • They Flocked to China for Boom Times. Now They’re Thinking Twice.
  • Australia’s Law Against Foreign Interference Gets Test Case
  • Looming Question for Putin Opponents: Can You Change Russia From Jail?
  • Facing Gas Shortages, Europe Seeks to Shrink Demand for Energy
  • Hong Kong: Mirror Concert Accident to Be Investigated
  • Your Friday Briefing
  • England Overhauls Medical Care for Transgender Youth
  • Why a Vogue Cover Created a Controversy for Olena Zelenska
  • Your Friday Briefing: Biden and Xi’s Fraught Phone Call
  • Russians, Risking Isolation in the South, Build Up for Ukrainian Attack
  • Seven Key Provisions in the Climate Deal
  • A.I. Predicts the Shape of Nearly Every Protein Known to Science
  • Zelensky Offers Passionate Speech for Ukraine’s Day of Statehood
  • Here Are Some Prisoner Swaps That Freed Americans
  • ‘Wagatha Christie’ Trial Verdict Expected on Friday
  • Joe Manchin and Chuck Schumer Have A Deal, for Now
  • An Indiana Doctor Speaks Out on Abortion, and Pays a Price
  • Los Angeles Enjoys Its New Bridge a Little Too Much
  • Justices Sotomayor and Barrett Say the Supreme Court Remains Collegial
  • Reversing a Reversal, Miami School Board Accepts Sex Ed Books It Had Rejected
  • Beating of Teen by Illinois Police Is Captured on Video
  • Guantánamo Prisoner Said He Was Waterboarded; Agents Omitted It From Memo
  • Doug Mastriano says his antisemitic ally ‘doesn’t speak for me’
  • Mike Pence Keeps Trying to Make Mike Pence Happen
  • Biden’s Granddaughter to Hold Wedding on White House’s South Lawn
  • F.B.I. Indicates It’s Willing to Settle Nassar Lawsuits
  • Eric Greitens Faces Ad Blitz and a Growing Threat to His Political Future
  • Tim Giago, Native American Newspaperman, Is Dead at 88
  • When the Heat Can’t Be Beat
  • Skeleton of a Gorgosaurus Sells at Auction for $6.1 Million
  • Is California Enjoying a Reprieve From Extreme Heat?
  • 4,000 Mistreated Beagles Need Homes. These Folks Are Stepping Up.
  • Inmates Allege They Were Raped and Assaulted After Guard Was Bribed
  • JetBlue and Spirit Airlines Announce Merger Plan
  • Trader Joe’s Workers at a Massachusetts Store Form a Union
  • Sheila Rayam Is Named Executive Editor of The Buffalo News
  • Apple Continues to Grow, but Profits Drop as Costs Rise

  • Amazon Posts Slowest Growth Rate in Two Decades

  • Treasury Secretary Yellen Said the U.S. Economy Was Transitioning to ‘Steady, Sustainable’ Growth

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 29 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 29 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-29 07:22AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Hong Kong national security law: 4 ex-members of defunct student activist group face up to 10 years in jail after admitting advocating for city’s ‘liberation’
  • Hair trigger: why Hong Kong schools are against schoolboys having long hair
  • ‘Abandon arrogance, face fact that national security law has restored stability in Hong Kong’: Beijing to UN Human Rights Committee on report proposing repeal of legislation
  • How much does it really cost to raise a child in Hong Kong?
  • Standard Chartered’s second-quarter profit rises 18 per cent, bucking global economic woes
  • Mirror concert mishap caused by snapped metal cord, Hong Kong officials reveal after giant screen crashes onto dancers on stage
  • Why cutting quarantine won’t be enough to revive Hong Kong’s economy: experts urge reopening of borders, full travel resumption
  • Coronavirus: Hong Kong could make Covid-19 oral drugs more accessible. Here’s what you need to know about the distribution of the pills
  • Hong Kong government slams ‘inaccurate’ report on city’s Covid-19 risk-exposure app by overseas cybersecurity firm
  • Officials seize more than 280 pieces of ivory from Hong Kong shop, biggest haul since trade banned last year
  • Inquiry ordered in Hong Kong after giant screen crashes down onto stage at Mirror concert, injuring 2 dancers; remaining shows cancelled
  • How Hong Kong became a cultural and commercial arts hub for Asia
  • Foodpanda to give HK$75,000 to family of Hong Kong rider killed in road accident
  • Hong Kong should align regulations with mainland China to promote health technology in Greater Bay Area, experts say
  • Hang Lung’s chairman warns of turbulent times ahead for Hong Kong’s real estate as city finds itself squeezed between US and China
  • Hong Kong home prices: worst yet to come, with up to 30 per cent slump a possibility as market enters decline
  • In a class of its own? Hong Kong University of Science and Technology to launch virtual reality lessons in bid to create metaverse campus
  • Chinese, Indian foreign ministers at Shanghai bloc summit as PLA ship heads to Sri Lanka
  • Delivery firm Missfresh collapses as another Nasdaq-listed Chinese firm falls prey to weakening economy
  • China’s Politburo to ensure delivery of unfinished homes to prevent mortgage-boycott crisis from escalating
  • Former Chinese police boss Fu Zhenghua admits corruption
  • Chinese solar firms ramping up investment in Southeast Asia to evade US, European trade tensions
  • Thailand says it won’t deport Uygurs to China amid crackdown on asylum seekers
  • A year after China’s private tutoring crackdown, classes have moved underground as companies struggle to pivot
  • What did Indonesia’s Jokowi hope to achieve with his whirlwind China-Japan-South Korea tour?
  • China food security: ‘astonishing’ food loss, wastefulness worsen grain situation, state media warns
  • China’s top leadership vows to give ‘green light’ to a batch of tech deals in sign of policy relaxation
  • Hangzhou market regulator summoned Meituan, Ele.me and other food delivery platforms over poor regulation and price-cutting
  • South China Sea law enforcement ship unveiled as Beijing seeks to tighten control over disputed waters
  • As chip-making business returns to US, Washington – and Arizona – dangle subsidies in race against China
  • US House approves Chips Act to subsidise and speed up semiconductor production
  • Risk of accidental US-China conflict over Taiwan could leave Japan badly exposed
  • Stay hydrated any place, any time: Chinese scientists develop a low-cost sponge that can suck water from air
  • China-US relations: Xi warns Biden on Taiwan, urging ‘clear-eyed’ approach versus playing with ‘fire’
  • China’s Politburo ‘insists’ on zero-Covid, omits mention of GDP growth target
  • China ramps up subsidies to lure chip firms to Hengqin, an island near Macau, turning it into a major semiconductor outpost
  • Japanese lawmakers visit Taiwan for talks on how to prepare for conflict
  • US, China urged to be ‘responsible’ global economic leaders in wake of Fed rate hike
  • China’s anti-corruption watchdog investigates minister leading tech drive
  • TikTok’s global expansion under a cloud as it faces renewed scrutiny over data privacy, China links
  • Singapore, New York see highest jump in rental prices this year, while Hong Kong sees decline
  • US, Japan discuss supply chains, semiconductors in ‘2-plus-2’ talks in face of China’s rise and Russia’s war
  • Australian TV soap ‘Neighbours’ that gave starts to Margo Robbie, Kylie Minogue, Russel Crowe, and Chris Hemsworth signs off after 37 years
  • South Korea steps closer to Ukraine war with massive arms deal to Poland
  • Malaysia bans party-hopping lawmakers to ease political turmoil
  • Malaysia’s Najib Razak hails Indonesia’s ‘proactive steps’ to deepen China ties
  • Joe Biden shrugs off recession talk, declares US ‘on the right path’
  • Pope Francis seeks forgiveness in Canada for sex abuse at residential schools
  • US Supreme Court Justice Alito mocks foreign critics in first public remarks since abortion ruling
  • First kisses may have helped spread cold sore virus
  • California court upholds death penalty for Hong Kong-born serial killer Charles Ng in 1980s sex slave murders
  • Russian journalist in TV protest fined again for discrediting army over Ukraine
  • Joe Biden is suddenly winning (just don’t say the ‘R’ word)
  • France’s Macron defies anger to welcome Saudi crown prince
  • Russia attacks Ukraine’s Kyiv area for the first time in weeks
  • Saudi crown prince stays in ‘world’s most expensive home’ ahead of Macron meeting
  • US economy shrinks in second quarter, raising recession fears
  • Wife of Russian who inspired Lord of War says arms dealer unaware of US prisoner swap offer
  • UK national security adviser warns of risk of miscalculating into nuclear war with China, Russia
  • Threat of ‘heatflation’ looms large as climate change shrinks farm and seafood output, experts say
  • Alibaba, Meituan sink Hong Kong stocks to 2-month low on regulatory pressure while China sticks to zero-Covid, slow-drip stimulus
  • Four Chinese companies ring the cowbell on SIX Swiss Exchange, marking landmark China-Zurich trading link
  • Macau invites casino operators to apply for licence renewals until September 24 to secure spots in the world’s gambling hub
  • Shimao, once hailed as one of China’s stronger developers, sued by UOB over terms of US$1.3 billion loan
  • How Hong Kong teachers’ mental health suffered from Covid-19 school closures, which caused 70pc to consider leaving teaching, new study finds
  • ‘The best saxophonist in Hong Kong’: jazz album by Timothy Sun showcases music by young composers in the city
  • Taron Egerton’s gritty role in Black Bird on Apple TV+ is worlds away from his turn as Elton John in Rocketman and a suave spy in Kingsman
  • Hotpot card game that plays on Cantonese slang and memes and the creators behind it, whose goal is to help preserve Hong Kong culture
  • A viral TikTok on why Stranger Things star should have Botox backfires – fans call nurse’s suggestions to Natalia Dyer ‘weird’
  • Ex-porn site owner who used nude photos of non-consenting subjects profiled in new Netflix documentary
  • K-drama review – Link: Eat, Love, Kill, Disney+ romcom-thriller mash-up, succeeds thanks to Moon Ga-young/Yeo Jin-goo chemistry
  • 5 new hotels in Kyoto, Niseko and Hakone being opened by Singapore’s Banyan Tree Group, its first in Japan
  • Peking duck, paella, May Chow’s baos – favourite Hong Kong restaurants of a food and beverage marketer
  • Why this Taiwan icon is next on our travel bucket list: the avant garde Taipei Performing Arts Centre could join monuments across the globe as a not-to-be-missed architectural attraction
  • 6 K-pop idols with tattoos who aren’t afraid to flaunt them, from BTS’ Jungkook, Got7’s Jackson Wang and Twice’s Chaeyoung, to Jay Park, Hyolyn and Hyuna – who matches with her fiancé Dawn
  • Meet new K-pop girl band NewJeans: Minji was in a BTS’ MV with Ed Sheeran, Haerin has been compared to Blackpink’s Jennie, and Hyein loves Harry Potter … but what about Hanni and Danielle?
  • STYLE Edit: Anna Hu’s debut at The European Fine Art Foundation (TEFAF) Maastricht – the Taiwan-born designer showcased dazzling art deco, flowers and music-themed high jewellery at the fine art fair
  • Would you splurge millions to live in a palace? Castles for sale in the Netherlands, Scotland and France, complete with moats and turrets to make you feel like a true royal
  • Meet Kim Kardashian’s low-key childhood bestie, Allison Statter: she’s the college dropout daughter of Irving Azoff, who’s managed J. Lo and Lizzo, and featured in Kim’s Skims and KKW campaigns
  • Has silicon won over Swiss watchmakers? Rolex, Jaeger-LeCoultre, Vacheron Constantin, Girard-Perregaux, IWC and Panerai are just a few brands on board with the revolutionary technology
  • Meet Antoine Arnault, billionaire Bernard Arnault’s eldest son: he runs Loro Piana and Berluti, is married to supermodel Natalia Vodianova and is a world-class poker player – could he inherit LVMH?
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.title: Korea Herald: 29 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • South Korean Navy to begin deploying women officers on submarines in 2024
  • Yoon calls for minimizing COVID-19 deaths while continuing return to normal
  • PPP lawmaker quits Supreme Council in apparent bid to switch to collective leadership system
  • S. Korea reports 193 cases of malaria so far this year
  • Yoon's approval rating sinks below 30% amid inflation, controversy over personnel appointments
  • S. Korean Navy to allow female sailors to serve as submarine personnel
  • [Photo News] Wall of Remembrance unveiled in Washington
  • [Graphic News] Number of international migrants falls at fastest pace in 2021 amid pandemic
  • S. Korea's new COVID-19 cases below 90,000 for 2nd day
  • NK leader says blood-sealed ties with China will develop for generations
  • USFK chief highlights importance of Korea-US alliance in dealing with challenges
  • N. Korea reports 3 new suspected COVID-19 cases: state media
  • US remains committed to defense of S. Korea, engagement with N. Korea: State Dept.
  • Ranking US official visits S. Korea to discuss cyber security: NSC
  • 3 candidates shortlisted for main opposition party's leadership race
  • Yoon, Widodo agree to cooperate on supply chains, capital relocation
  • Population shock: Korea reports first decline on record in 2021
  • Is Korea soft on crime?
  • Korea launches Jeongjo the Great, Navy's first 8,200-ton Aegis destroyer
  • Krafton’s popular battle-royale game banned in India
  • Posco secures $1b through overseas debt sale
  • Hyundai Motor names new CEO for US operations
  • POSCO raises $1b via overseas debt sale
  • Samsung SDI logs best-ever results in Q2 on robust EV battery sales
  • Korean Air to resume routes to Rome and Barcelona in Sept.
  • Hanwha Life Insurance Q2 net income up 92.8% to W318.6b
  • SK Innovation Q2 net profit up 589.8% to W1.33tr
  • Widodo asks Hyundai Motor to join capital relocation project
  • LG Chem, GS Caltex join hands to build white bio ecosystem
  • [Newsmaker] Starbucks Korea admits giveaway bags had cancer-causing chemicals
  • SK On to attract $2b loan to back 3rd battery plant in Europe
  • Posco Chemical signs W13tr cathode deal with GM
  • Seoul shares extend winning streak to 5th day on hope for Fed's less aggressive rake hikes
  • Yoon to slash payroll at public firms in 2023
  • S. Korea seeks to overhaul public firms to prevent lax management
  • Card spending climbs 15% in Q2 on eased COVID-19 curbs
  • Tax revenue up W36.5tr in H1 amid economic recovery
  • Exports of passenger cars rise 2.5% in H1
  • Seoul shares open higher on expectations over Fed's less aggressive rate hikes
  • S. Korea's consumption falls for 4th month amid high inflation
  • Concert marking Liberation Day to be held at Cheong Wa Dae
  • Unique summer getaway at BTS: In The Soop Estate
  • [Well-curated weekend] Paddle on Han, watch outdoor screenings of performances and catch an exhibition at museum storage
  • [Around the Hotels] Promotions and packages
  • [Travel Bits] Festivals, sights across Korea

entry.content:

The Asahi Shimbun is widely regarded for its journalism as the most respected daily newspaper in Japan. The English version offers selected articles from the vernacular Asahi Shimbun, as well as extensive coverage of cool Japan,focusing on manga, travel and other timely news.
